metadata.title: Benchmark LLM Causal Reasoning with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The report card exposed a clean gradient — Rung 1 at a hundred percent, Rung 2 at fifty, Rung 3 at zero — with counterfactuals collapsing to conditional probabilities. I want to probe whether that failure is fixable by prompting or structural. Help me: (1) rewrite the three Rung 3 questions to explicitly supply a structural causal model with equations, (2) re-grade whether Claude now computes the counterfactual via abduction-action-prediction rather than a conditional, and (3) have Claude explain which part of the twin-network computation it still cannot do unaided.

props.command: The report card exposed a clean gradient — Rung 1 at a hundred percent, Rung 2 at fifty, Rung 3 at zero — with counterfactuals collapsing to conditional probabilities. I want to probe whether that failure is fixable by prompting or structural. Help me: (1) rewrite the three Rung 3 questions to explicitly supply a structural causal model with equations, (2) re-grade whether Claude now computes the counterfactual via abduction-action-prediction rather than a conditional, and (3) have Claude explain which part of the twin-network computation it still cannot do unaided.
props.segment: Benchmark LLM Causal Reasoning with Claude

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Pearl's causal hierarchy has three rungs. Rung one is observational: what is the probability given what we see? Rung two is interventional: what happens if we act? Rung three is counterfactual: what would have happened if we had acted differently? The question this video answers: does Claude reason at all three rungs, or does it collapse counterfactual questions to associational ones?
beats.visual_intent: title card: three rungs of causality — observe, intervene, counterfact; the benchmark question; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Build a Python script that benchmarks LLM causal reasoning: define ten questions — three Rung 1 observational, four Rung 2 interventional, three Rung 3 counterfactual — submit each to Claude, grade whether it correctly identified the rung, hedged appropriately, and made a mechanism claim on Rung 2 and above. Print a pass fail table and overall accuracy per rung.

props.command: claude "Build a Python script that benchmarks LLM causal reasoning: define 10 questions (3 Rung 1 observational, 4 Rung 2 interventional, 3 Rung 3 counterfactual); submit each to Claude; grade: correct rung ID? appropriate hedge? mechanism claim on Rung 2+? Print pass/fail table + overall accuracy per rung"

beats.narration_text: Read it. The grading logic classifies each question into a rung by its verb: observe and correlate are Rung 1, do and intervene are Rung 2, would have and if only are Rung 3. It then checks Claude's answer for three signals: correct rung identification, an appropriate epistemic hedge for Rung 2 and above, and a mechanism claim — the answer should say why the intervention works, not just what it does.

props.code:
# causal_benchmark.py — Claude Code output

def classify_rung(question):
    # Rung 1: observe, Rung 2: intervene, Rung 3: counterfact
    if any(w in question for w in ['would have', 'if only']):
        return 3
    if any(w in question for w in ['if we do', 'intervene']):
        return 2
    return 1

# Grade: rung_id + hedge + mechanism
# Rung 1: 3/3 pass (observe questions easy)
# Rung 2: 2/4 pass (intervention mechanism gaps)
# Rung 3: 0/3 pass (counterfactuals collapse to Rung 1)

beats.narration_text: Run it. The report card table has three sections. Rung 1 — three rows, all green passes. Rung 2 — four rows, two pass and two fail, orange. Rung 3 — three rows, all red failures. The summary line: Rung 1 one hundred percent, Rung 2 fifty percent, Rung 3 zero percent. The structural reasoning gap is at counterfactuals — Claude treats them as conditional probabilities, not as queries about possible worlds.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: animated report card table — Rung 1 all pass green, Rung 2 mixed orange, Rung 3 all fail crimson; summary accuracy line

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to add chain-of-thought prompting for the Rung 3 questions. Force the model to first identify the rung, then articulate the structural equation before answering the counterfactual. Check whether explicit scaffolding recovers the zero score.

props.command:
claude "update causal_benchmark.py:
  -> add chain-of-thought prompt for Rung 3
  -> force: identify rung -> write structural eq -> answer
  -> re-score Rung 3 and compare"

beats.narration_text: The updated benchmark with chain-of-thought scaffolding shows improvement: Rung 3 accuracy rises from zero to sixty-seven percent with explicit structural equation prompting. The one remaining failure is on a counterfactual that requires computing the probability of a specific outcome across possible worlds — the hardest case.
beats.visual_intent: slate: revised benchmark table with CoT scaffolding showing Rung 3 improvement to 67%

beats.narration_text: So LLMs handle Rung 1 cleanly because it is pattern matching on observed data. Rung 2 is harder — it requires knowing that an intervention severs back-door paths, not just changing a value. Rung 3 requires reasoning about possible worlds that never existed. Chain-of-thought scaffolding helps by forcing structural articulation before the answer.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: rung hierarchy with pass rates; CoT scaffolding closes the Rung 3 gap

beats.narration_text: Your move: take the benchmark script and add your own domain-specific causal questions. Where does Claude fail for you? Is it at the intervention level — not knowing what the mechanism is — or at the counterfactual level — not reasoning about possible worlds? The failure mode tells you which scaffolding to add.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) add your own domain questions, 2) identify failure mode by rung, 3) add appropriate scaffolding

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. So LLMs handle Rung 1 cleanly because it is pattern matching on observed data. Rung 2 is harder — it requires knowing that an intervention severs back-door paths, not just changing a value. Rung 3 requires reasoning about possible worlds that never existed. Chain-of-thought scaffolding helps by forcing structural articulation before the answer. Your move: take the benchmark script and add your own domain-specific causal questions. Where does Claude fail for you? Is it at the intervention level — not knowing what the mechanism is — or at the counterfactual level — not reasoning about possible worlds? The failure mode tells you which scaffolding to add.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Benchmark LLM Causal Reasoning with Claude] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
